M1 2SIA · 2025–2026

Football Performance
Monitoring

Building a GPS/IMU data pipeline for physical load analysis in professional football.

Celine BENDEKOUM
Python-R-Git
Denis MOTTET
April 2026
University of Montpellier
Master Sport, Health and Artificial Intelligence
github.com/celinebenstudent/Football_Analysis

Section 01Scientific Question

Modern football clubs equip players with GPS/IMU sensors that record position, speed, and acceleration at every session. This generates large volumes of time-series data that need to be aggregated, quality-controlled, and interpreted rapidly.

The central question of this project is: Can we build a fully reproducible, SQL-queryable monitoring pipeline that answers — automatically and consistently — the key load-management questions a coaching staff asks every week?

Core question 1
Match vs training load
Volume · Intensity · Mechanical
Core question 2
Positional differences
Who runs the most? Where?
Core question 3
Atypical sessions
Outlier detection · Data quality
Core question 4
Player comparison
Profile · Normalized KPIs

Section 02Data

Sources

Three data sources are used, all stored under data/data/ and centralised into a single football.duckdb database at the project root:

File Format Content Used for
football.duckdb DuckDB database Persistent SQL database containing all ingested tables and views (summary_raw, summary, players, summary_clean). Generated by main.ipynb on first run. Central storage for all Python analyses. Excluded from Git via .gitignore.
summary.csv CSV One row per player × session. Aggregated KPIs per GPS sensor: total distance, 7 speed zones, max speed, average speed, accelerations and decelerations ≥ 3 m/s². Primary source for all team-level analyses, statistical tests (R), and player comparisons.
Raw tracking files CSV per sensor Position and speed time series at fine temporal resolution, one file per sensor × session. Cover both game and practice sessions. Available for within-session analysis. Not used in the current pipeline version.

Key variables in summary.csv

Original columnRenamed toMeaningUnit
feature_distancedistance_kmTotal distance covered in the sessionkm
feature_max_speedmax_speed_kmhPeak instantaneous speed recordedkm/h
feature_avg_speedavg_speed_kmhAverage speed over active timekm/h
feature_d_speed_0_5 … over30d_0_5_km … d_over30_kmDistance in each of the 7 speed zones (0–5, 5–10, 10–15, 15–21, 21–24, 24–30, >30 km/h)km
feature_acc_3mssacc_3mssCount of acceleration efforts ≥ 3 m/s²count
feature_dec_3mssdec_3mssCount of deceleration efforts ≥ 3 m/s²count
typesession_typeSession category: game or practice—
SensorsensorUnique integer ID of the GPS device worn by the player—
positionpositionTactical position assigned to the player for the session—

Data quality rules

Seven rules are enforced in the summary_clean SQL view (Python) and mirrored identically in main.Rmd (R), so both pipelines operate on the same clean dataset. Rules apply to either all sessions or matches only, as indicated below.

RuleConditionScopeRationale
Valid sensor & timestamp sensor IS NOT NULL
session_ts IS NOT NULL		 All Rows without a sensor ID or date cannot be linked to a player or period and are unusable for monitoring.
Known session type session_type IN ('game', 'practice') All Only the two session categories relevant to this tool are retained; other values are excluded.
No negative KPI values distance_km ≥ 0
max_speed_kmh ≥ 0
acc_3mss ≥ 0
dec_3mss ≥ 0		 All Physical quantities cannot be negative. A negative value is unambiguously a sensor export error.
Match minimum distance distance_km ≥ 0.5 Games only Values below 0.5 km in a match correspond to unused substitutes or sensors that failed to record. They would distort team averages.
Match maximum distance distance_km ≤ 20.0 Games only No outfield player covers more than 20 km in a 90-minute match. Higher values indicate a GPS artefact.
Plausible max speed max_speed_kmh ≤ 40.0 Games only The absolute human sprint record is ~44 km/h. Values above 40 km/h in a team sport context indicate sensor noise or GPS multi-path errors.
Speed-zone coherence Σ zones ≤ distance_km × 1.15 All The sum of the seven zone distances cannot substantially exceed the total. A 15 % tolerance covers GPS rounding; beyond this the record is internally inconsistent.

Section 03Python Pipeline

Python DuckDB / SQL Entry point: main.ipynb

All data flows through football.duckdb — the DuckDB database created at startup. Every analysis queries this file via SQL, which guarantees that KPIs and filters are defined once and reused consistently throughout the notebook.

Architecture overview

1
Ingestion — summary_raw

The CSV is imported entirely as VARCHAR with read_csv_auto(all_varchar=true). This prevents any import failure due to bad types or missing values. All NA, NaN, N/A and empty strings are mapped to SQL NULL.

2
Typed view — summary

A SQL view applies try_cast to convert each column to its correct type (DOUBLE, TIMESTAMP, INTEGER). If a value cannot be cast, it silently becomes NULL instead of breaking the pipeline — making the system robust by design.

3
Player dimension — players

Associates each sensor ID with a player name. An anonymization toggle (ANONYMIZE_NAMES = True) replaces real names with PLAYER_001, PLAYER_002, … for data privacy.

4
Clean view — summary_clean

Applies the quality rules described in Section 2. All downstream analyses use this view exclusively, guaranteeing consistent KPI definitions across every query.

Key code — ingestion pattern

SQL (DuckDB) 
-- Step 1: robust raw import (never fails on bad values)
CREATE TABLE summary_raw AS
SELECT * FROM read_csv_auto(
  'data/data/summary.csv',
  header=true,
  all_varchar=true,             -- everything is text at this stage
  nullstr=['NA','NaN','N/A','']   -- map common NA patterns to NULL
);

-- Step 2: typed view — try_cast avoids pipeline crashes
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW summary AS
SELECT
  try_cast("Sensor"           AS INTEGER)   AS sensor,
  try_cast("date"             AS TIMESTAMP) AS session_ts,
  try_cast("feature_distance" AS DOUBLE)    AS distance_km,
  try_cast("feature_max_speed"AS DOUBLE)    AS max_speed_kmh,
  -- ... other columns follow the same pattern
FROM summary_raw;

Reading everything as text first (all_varchar=true) prevents the import from failing on a single malformed cell. The try_cast in the next view then converts each column to its correct type — any value that cannot be cast silently becomes NULL instead of crashing the pipeline.

Key code — quality filter

SQL (DuckDB) 
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW summary_clean AS
WITH s AS (
  SELECT *, -- compute zone sum for coherence check
    (coalesce(d_0_5_km,0)  + coalesce(d_5_10_km,0)  +
     coalesce(d_10_15_km,0) + coalesce(d_15_21_km,0) +
     coalesce(d_21_24_km,0) + coalesce(d_24_30_km,0) +
     coalesce(d_over30_km,0)) AS zones_sum_km
  FROM summary
)
SELECT * FROM s
WHERE
  distance_km BETWEEN 0.5 AND 20.0   -- match: must have actually played
  AND max_speed_kmh <= 40.0           -- physiologically plausible
  AND zones_sum_km <= distance_km * 1.15; -- internal coherence

The WITH s AS (...) clause pre-computes the zone sum so it can be compared to distance_km in a single pass. Using a view (not a table) means the filter is re-evaluated automatically whenever new data is loaded — no manual refresh needed.

Analyses performed

🐍
Match — Team overview

Computes KPIs per match date (team total distance, HID, accelerations, max speed average and peak). Produces time-series plots of load evolution over the season and stacked bar charts of speed-zone distributions. Identifies the most and least demanding sessions. Boxplots by position reveal structural role differences (midfielders vs defenders vs forwards).

🐍
Training — Team overview + specific session

Same structure as the match analysis applied to practice sessions. A specific intense training session is isolated and its player-level profile is examined in detail.

🐍
Dashboard + Individual comparison

A "dashboard" table summarizes medians and IQR for all KPIs split by session type. Two players are compared with normalized KPIs — each player's values are divided by their own seasonal average, making the comparison independent of total playing time.

Derived KPI: High Intensity Distance (HID)

Python 
# HID = distance run above 21 km/h (high-intensity threshold in football)
# This is the most operationally relevant KPI for fatigue management
df['hid_km'] = (df['d_21_24_km'] + df['d_24_30_km'] + df['d_over30_km'])

# HID ratio: share of total load that is "high intensity"
# Two players with same total distance but different HID ratio
# have experienced very different physical demands
df['hid_ratio'] = df['hid_km'] / df['distance_km']
Figure 3.1 — Daily team total distance (km) split by session type (2019–2021). Match load (orange) is remarkably stable around 100–115 km/day, while training load (blue) is more variable, reflecting periodization. The flat period in mid-2020 corresponds to the COVID-19 suspension.
Figure 3.2 — Match monitoring dashboard: (top-left) team total distance per match; (top-right) HID share (≥21 km/h) — typically 3.5–5 %; (bottom-left) team accelerations and decelerations ≥3 m/s²; (bottom-right) mean and peak max speed. These four KPIs together characterise both the volume and intensity of each match.
Figure 3.3 — Top 10 players by distance in the match of 2020-08-06. PLAYER_015 (center midfield) leads at ~15.5 km, followed by several outfield players in the 14 km range. This session-level view lets staff instantly identify who bore the highest physical load on matchday.
Figure 3.4 — Distance vs HID ratio scatter for the training session of 2020-01-13. Each dot is a player coloured by position. Left forwards and center forwards tend to sit higher on the HID axis at similar distances, revealing role-specific intensity profiles within a single session.
Figure 3.5 — Average speed-zone profile across all sessions for two players. PLAYER_028 dedicates a larger share of distance to the 10–15 and 15–21 km/h zones, suggesting a more "running-intensive" profile, while both players are comparable in the low-speed zones (0–10 km/h).
Figure 3.6 — Effort density (acc + dec ≥ 3 m/s² per km) for both players split by session type. PLAYER_028 consistently produces more efforts per km than PLAYER_011 in both contexts, indicating a more explosive mechanical profile regardless of session type.

Section 04R Statistical Analyses

R ggplot2 rstatix Entry point: main.Rmd

The R analysis focuses on inferential statistics and model diagnostics — tasks where R's native statistical ecosystem (non-parametric tests, linear models, residuals plots) provides a natural advantage over Python. The same cleaning thresholds as the Python pipeline are re-applied in R for consistency.

A. Normality check — Shapiro–Wilk + QQ-plots

Before choosing a test, normality of the distance variable is assessed for each session type using the Shapiro–Wilk test and QQ-plots. Both distributions are non-normal (heavy tails, p < 0.001), justifying the use of non-parametric tests.

R 
# QQ-plot to visually assess normality
qqnorm(dist_game, main="QQ-plot — Game", col="#E74C3C")
qqline(dist_game, col="#C0392B", lwd=2)

# Shapiro-Wilk formal test (H0: data are normally distributed)
shapiro.test(sample(dist_game, min(length(dist_game), 5000)))

The sample() cap at 5 000 is required by Shapiro–Wilk. The QQ-plot provides a visual first check; the formal test gives the p-value. If p < 0.05 normality is rejected and a non-parametric test is used.

B. Wilcoxon rank-sum test — game vs practice

The Wilcoxon test (non-parametric equivalent of the t-test) compares the distribution of distance, HID, max speed, and accelerations between match and training sessions. A Benjamini–Hochberg correction is applied for multiple comparisons.

R 
# Wilcoxon test with effect size (r = Z / sqrt(N))
wt <- wilcox.test(distance_km ~ session_type, data=df, conf.int=TRUE)
es <- df |> wilcox_effsize(distance_km ~ session_type)

Wilcoxon compares rank distributions — no normality assumption needed. conf.int = TRUE returns a confidence interval on the location shift. The effect size r = Z / √N separates statistical from practical significance.

R
# Multiple KPIs with BH correction
vars <- c("HID_km", "max_speed", "acc_3mss")
results <- lapply(vars, \(v) {
  wt <- wilcox.test(as.formula(paste(v, "~ session_type")), data=df)
  tibble(variable=v, W=wt$statistic, p_value=wt$p.value)
}) |> bind_rows() |>
  mutate(p_adj = p.adjust(p_value, method="BH"))

lapply iterates over the three KPIs, returning one result row each. Benjamini–Hochberg ("BH") controls the false discovery rate across multiple tests — less conservative than Bonferroni while still limiting spurious findings.

C. Kruskal–Wallis + Dunn post-hoc — position differences

A Kruskal–Wallis test checks whether total distance differs significantly across positions. A Dunn post-hoc test with Bonferroni correction identifies which pairs of positions are significantly different.

D. Linear regression model

A linear model estimates how well max_speed and HID_km predict total distance, controlling for session type. The four diagnostic plots (residuals vs fitted, QQ-residuals, scale-location, Cook's distance) are used to assess model assumptions.

R 
# Model: distance ~ max_speed + HID + session_type
model <- lm(distance_km ~ max_speed + HID_km + session_type,
           data = df_model)
summary(model)

# Residuals diagnostics — 4 plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model, which = 1:4)

factor(session_type) creates a binary dummy — its coefficient captures the match vs practice distance gap net of speed and HID. par(mfrow = c(2,2)) lays out all four diagnostics on one figure.

E. Outlier detection via z-score

Within each position × session-type group, sessions where a player's distance deviates by more than 2 standard deviations from the group mean are flagged as anomalies. This provides a simple, operationally useful alert system for the coaching staff.

R 
# Flag sessions where |z-score| > 2 within position × session type
df_flagged <- df |>
  group_by(session_type, position) |>
  mutate(
    group_mean = mean(distance_km, na.rm=TRUE),
    group_sd   = sd(distance_km,   na.rm=TRUE),
    z_score    = (distance_km - group_mean) / group_sd,
    flag       = abs(z_score) > 2    # TRUE = anomaly
  ) |>
  ungroup()

group_by(session_type, position) scopes the z-score to each position × session-type group — a goalkeeper is compared only to other goalkeepers. ungroup() is called immediately after to prevent the grouping propagating silently into downstream steps.

HID_km sums the three highest speed zones (21–24, 24–30, >30 km/h). Dividing by distance_km gives a dimensionless ratio that makes two players with different total distances directly comparable in terms of relative intensity.

Figure 4.1 — Violin + boxplot of distance (km) by session type. Match median ≈ 8.7 km vs practice ≈ 5.6 km. The wider violin for games reflects greater variability due to substitutions and role diversity.
Figure 4.2 — Residuals diagnostics for the linear model. The Residuals vs Fitted plot shows mild heteroscedasticity at low fitted values. Cook's distance identifies a small number of influential observations (obs. 3310, 2878) that warrant manual inspection.

Visualisations from main.Rmd

Figure 4.3 — Team average distance per session across 2019–2021 (LOESS, 95 % CI). Match load (red) stays stable near 8 km/player. Training load (blue) rises from ~4.5 km in July 2019 to ~5.5 km by early 2020, drops during COVID suspension, then recovers in the 2020–21 season.
Figure 4.4 — Mean distance per speed zone by session type. Games produce more distance at 10–15 and 15–21 km/h (running and high-intensity running). Training shows relatively more distance at 0–5 km/h, reflecting time spent in positional or technical work at low intensity.
Figure 4.5 — Distance by position and session type. Every position shows a game > practice gap, most pronounced for midfield and forward roles. The wide IQR in games across all positions confirms high intra-position variability, consistent with differences in playing time and game context.
Figure 4.6 — QQ-plots of distance for game (red, left) and practice (blue, right). The game distribution shows a clear S-shaped departure from the theoretical line confirming non-normality. Practice is closer to normal but also fails Shapiro–Wilk (p < 0.001), which justifies using the Wilcoxon test for both groups.

Section 05Results

5.1 Dataset after quality control

Match rows
1 011
player × session
Training rows
3 030
player × session
Players tracked
29
unique GPS sensors
Data retention
> 95 %
rows pass QC rules

Quality control (QC) removes fewer than 5 % of rows — mainly GPS dropouts and unused-substitute entries in matches — confirming good overall sensor reliability across the 2019–2021 seasons.

5.2 Match load vs training load

The Wilcoxon rank-sum test shows that every physical key performance indicator (KPI) is significantly higher in matches than in training sessions (all p < 0.001, large effect sizes).

Total distance
+42 %
~8.7 km game vs ~5.6 km practice
HID — High Intensity Distance (≥21 km/h)
+60 %
~0.31 km game vs ~0.07 km practice
Acc/Dec efforts (≥3 m/s²)
+25 %
significantly more in matches

What this means operationally: a player experiences roughly 1.6× more high-intensity running and 1.25× more explosive acceleration/deceleration efforts in a match compared to a typical training session. This gap should directly inform post-match recovery planning.

5.3 Positional differences — Kruskal–Wallis + Dunn post-hoc

Total distance differs significantly across positions (Kruskal–Wallis H ≈ 85, p < 0.001). The Dunn post-hoc test (Bonferroni correction) identifies the sharpest contrasts:

ComparisonDirectionInterpretation
Midfielders vs central defenders MF > CD Midfielders cover more total distance — consistent with their box-to-box role.
Wide players vs central defenders Wide > CD Wider roles involve more running along the flanks, increasing total volume.
Goalkeepers vs all outfield positions GK << All Goalkeepers form a statistically distinct group with substantially lower values for all outfield KPIs.

5.4 Linear regression model

The model predicts total distance from HID, max speed, and session type.

R² — variance explained
≈ 0.72
strong overall fit
RMSE — Root Mean Square Error
≈ 0.9 km
typical prediction error
Top predictors
HID & type
both p < 0.001

The residual diagnostics reveal mild heteroscedasticity (non-constant variance) at low fitted values and a small number of influential observations. This suggests that a log-transformation of the outcome or a linear mixed-effects model — accounting for repeated measures per player — would improve the fit in a future version.

5.5 Outlier detection

Approximately 5 % of rows are flagged as anomalies (|z-score| > 2 within their position × session-type group). Two main causes are identified upon visual inspection:

Cause 1
Injury returns
Players with restricted minutes post-injury show abnormally low distances within their positional group.
Cause 2
GPS dropouts
Sensor failures produce near-zero distance values unrelated to actual physical output.

All flagged sessions require human validation before any operational decision is made. The z-score alert is a screening tool, not a definitive diagnosis.

Section 06Discussion

Main findings

Finding 1 — SQL-first architecture works for monitoring. Defining all KPIs once in a DuckDB view and reusing them across Python and R ensures perfectly consistent results. Any future addition of data (new season, new sensors) requires no code change in the analysis layer.
Finding 2 — Game vs practice load is large and systematic. Match distance and high-intensity distance are substantially higher than in training across all positions. This gap must be accounted for in weekly load planning.
Finding 3 — Positional benchmarks are actionable. The Kruskal–Wallis analysis produces position-specific reference values. A player whose distance falls 2 SD below their positional median is a reliable early warning signal for fatigue or incomplete recovery.

Limitations

Limit 1 — Quality thresholds are calibrated by domain knowledge, not data. The choice of 40 km/h as max-speed cut-off and 0.5 km as minimum match distance is physiologically reasonable but not validated against an external ground truth.
Limit 2 — The linear model ignores player-level autocorrelation. Repeated measures per player create within-player correlation that OLS cannot handle. A linear mixed-effects model with a random intercept per player would be more appropriate.
Limit 3 — No playing-time information. Without minutes played, it is impossible to distinguish a substitute with low distance from an underperforming starter. The monitoring value of the tool would be greatly enhanced by adding this variable.

Perspectives

Two directions stand out for extending this work. First, integrating individual playing time would substantially improve the validity of inter-player comparisons — a starter covering 10 km and a 15-minute substitute are currently indistinguishable from distance alone. Second, the SQL-first architecture makes the pipeline straightforward to generalise to other team sports (rugby, handball, basketball): only the threshold values and speed-zone boundaries in the view definitions would need to be updated, leaving the analytical and statistical layers intact.
